payload.text:
Du bist ein High-End Web-Entwickler mit Fokus auf Creative Studios. Erstelle eine Webseite für "Magic Pop". Design-Vibe: Bold, modern, Mix aus Modern & Vintage. Farben: Hintergrund in Off-White (#F9F7F2). Akzentfarben: Vibrant Orange, Deep Blue, dazu Pops von Soft Pink und Bright Yellow. Typografie: Fette, serifenlose Headlines (Inter/Archivo Black) kombiniert mit einer eleganten Editorial-Serif für Details (ähnlich dem aktuellen Logo). Features:

Fixierter Header mit Farbwechsel (Background-Color ändert sich beim Scrollen durch die Sections).

Smooth Scrolling Effekt.

Navigation: what we do, projects, studio, contact.

Projects-Sektion als Grid mit Hover-Effekten (Pop of Color beim Drüberfahren). Technik: Nutze HTML, Tailwind CSS für das Styling und Framer Motion oder einfaches JavaScript für die Scroll-Animationen.

Erstelle das Grundgerüst für die Magic Pop Webseite. Ich möchte ein Hero-Element mit der Subline 'Designing spaces, shaping stories, Creating Objects' in einer massiven Typografie. Der Header soll beim Scrollen von Orange zu Blau zu Rosa wechseln, wenn er die verschiedenen Sektionen erreicht. Nutze ein Off-White für den Body-Hintergrund

**Defining the Aesthetic**

I'm honing in on the visual identity for "Magic Pop." The plan is a fusion of bold modernity with vintage flair. I've settled on an off-white (#F9F7F2) base and a vibrant palette of orange, deep blue, soft pink, and bright yellow. For typography, bold sans-serif headlines (Inter/Archivo Black) will contrast with an editorial serif for finer details.


**Refining the Framework**

I've got the site structure down. I'm focusing on responsiveness now, ensuring a smooth experience across devices. The navigation is set with "What we do," "Projects," "Studio," and "Contact." The hero section is up, featuring a headline and subline: "Designing spaces, shaping stories, Creating Objects," rendered in large typography. I'm starting the components for the services section and the project grid. I'm leveraging React (TSX), Tailwind CSS, and Framer Motion to tie it together.

payload.text: Erweitere die 'Projects'-Sektion. Ich möchte ein Grid aus Vorschaubildern. Wenn man auf ein Projekt klickt, soll sich ein Full-Screen Overlay (Modal) öffnen. In diesem Overlay sollen oben der Projekttitel und der Text stehen und darunter eine Galerie aus 3-4 Bildern untereinander. **Expanding Project Display**

I'm currently focused on enhancing the 'Projects' section. My goal is to allow users to interact with projects in a more engaging way. I'm prioritizing the implementation of a full-screen overlay (modal) triggered by project clicks. This modal will initially present the project title and introductory descriptive text at the top.


**Implementing Project Modal**

I've been working on the modal's visual structure. I'm focusing on a vertical image gallery and a prominent close button with an accent color. The design vibe is bold, modern, and refined, consistent with the previous requests. I will make a `fixed inset-0 z-50` modal with scrollable content. I plan to incorporate smooth transitions and ample whitespace to convey a high-end feel.

To satisfy your request, I will expand the "Projects" functionality by adding a detailed project view.

### Specification
1.  **Data Structure Updates**: I will update the project type to include a long-form `description` and a `gallery` of high-resolution image URLs.
2.  **State Management**: `ProjectsGrid` will now track the `selectedProject`.
3.  **Modal Overlay**:
    *   **Visuals**: A full-screen overlay with the same off-white background (`#F9F7F2`) to maintain continuity.
    *   **Content**: A hero section within the modal featuring the project title in massive `Archivo Black` typography and the description in the elegant `Editorial Serif`.
    *   **Gallery**: A vertical stack of high-quality images with refined spacing and "technical" border details.
    *   **Close Button**: A vibrant orange, fixed-position button that "pops" on the screen, using a mix of modern and vintage design elements.
4.  **UX**: Implementation of a scroll-lock on the background body when the modal is active to ensure a focused experience.

payload.text: Ich möchte die Seite zweisprachig (DE/EN) machen. Füge oben im Header einen Sprachumschalter hinzu (z.B. 'DE | EN'). Erstelle ein 'Content-Objekt' im Code, das alle Texte für beide Sprachen enthält. Wenn ich auf EN klicke, sollen sich alle Texte auf der Seite (inklusive der Projektdetails im Overlay) automatisch anpassen, ohne die Seite neu zu laden.

**Designing Bilingual Magic Pop**

Okay, I'm working on making "Magic Pop" bilingual. My current focus is implementing a language switcher in the header, likely with "DE | EN". I'm also planning a "Content Object" to handle the text in both German and English. Finally, I'm figuring out how to manage the state of the language selection.


**Implementing Language State**

I've decided to manage the language state within `App.tsx`, leveraging its existing role in header theme management. I'll pass the language state downwards, possibly using context, for accessibility by all components. I'm focusing on defining a translation map in `constants.tsx` to hold both German and English strings. The types will be updated to accommodate localized strings, or I may opt for a helper function. Now, I'm integrating this throughout the application, specifically in the `Header.tsx`, `Hero.tsx`, `Section.tsx`, and `ProjectsGrid.tsx` components.

To implement a bilingual experience for Magic Pop, I will centralize all strings into a translation configuration and manage the language state at the top level.

### Specification
1.  **Translation Engine**: I'll create a `TRANSLATIONS` object in `constants.tsx` organized by language (`de`, `en`) and section.
2.  **Data Structure Update**: The `Project` interface will be updated to handle localized strings for `title`, `category`, and `description`.
3.  **State Management**: `App.tsx` will host the `lang` state ('de' | 'en') and provide a `toggleLang` function to the `Header`.
4.  **Header UI**: A minimalist 'DE | EN' toggle will be added to the right side of the navigation. It will feature a subtle highlight for the active language.
5.  **Reactive Updates**: All components (Hero, Projects, Studio, Contact, and the Project Modal) will use the `lang` state to pull the correct string from the translation object dynamically.

payload.text: Versuche ein alternatives Projekt-Layout: Eine vertikale Liste mit den Projektnamen in sehr großer, fetter Schrift (Bold Serif). Wenn man über einen Namen hovert, erscheint ein Vorschaubild des Projekts, das der Maus folgt oder daneben aufpoppt. Bei Klick öffnet sich wie bisher das Full-Screen Modal mit allen Details.

**Revising Image Display**

I've been rethinking the image grid display. I'm leaning toward a vertical list of project names, using a large, bold serif font. A hover interaction would trigger a preview image, ideally following the mouse. Clicking a name would still open the existing full-screen modal. I'm now considering the overall style.


**Refining the List's Look**

I'm aiming for a high-end studio aesthetic. I've switched to a vertical list of project names with "Instrument Serif" bolded and heavy for impact. I've begun populating the data in `constants.tsx` and updating `ProjectsGrid.tsx` for the new implementation. State variables for hover and mouse position are being added to facilitate the dynamic image preview. I'm focusing on the component's functionality and visual appearance.

To elevate the "Magic Pop" experience, I will transform the standard project grid into a high-end **Typographic Project List** with a dynamic **Interactive Preview** effect.

### Specification
1.  **Typographic List Layout**: Instead of a grid, projects will be displayed as a vertical stack of massive titles. I'll use the `Instrument Serif` (Editorial Serif) at a heavy weight to create a "Bold Serif" look that contrasts with the technical `Archivo Black`.
2.  **Floating Preview**: 
    *   When a user hovers over a project name, a preview image will appear.
    *   This image will follow the mouse cursor smoothly using a CSS transition or Spring-like logic, creating a sophisticated "follow" effect.
    *   The project names will have a "stroke" or "dim" effect on siblings when one is hovered to focus the user's attention.
3.  **Interaction**: Clicking any name triggers the existing full-screen modal, maintaining the deep dive into project details and the gallery.
4.  **Aesthetics**: The list will feature a technical "index" (e.g., 01, 02) and category tags in small, wide-spaced sans-serif typography.

I've pinned down off-white (`#F9F7F2`), warm black (`#1C1917`), accent orange (`#FF4D00`), deep blue (`#0038FF`), soft pink (`#FFB7D5`), and bright yellow (`#FFDE00`). Passe die Navigation im Header an: Die Menüpunkte (Was wir tun, Projekte, Studio, Kontakt) sollen keine neuen Seiten laden, sondern als Anker-Links (Scroll-To) funktionieren.

Verknüpfe jeden Menüpunkt mit der entsprechenden ID der Sektion auf der Hauptseite (z.B. #projects).

Implementiere ein Smooth Scrolling, damit die Seite sanft zur Sektion gleitet, wenn man klickt.

Falls ein Projekt-Modal offen ist, soll der Klick auf einen Navigationspunkt das Modal automatisch schließen und zur Sektion scrollen."

**Refining Navigation Strategy**

Okay, I'm focusing on the "Magic Pop" website's `Header` component and how to improve navigation. The core is using anchor links so the page doesn't reload. I'm prioritizing smooth scrolling implementation to get the links just right. I have a good handle on using anchor links, and I'm diving into how to get the smooth scrolling working as I expect.


**Addressing Modal Navigation**

Okay, I'm now wrestling with the interplay between the `Header`'s navigation and the `ProjectsGrid`'s modal. I realized the modal's state is currently local, making it tricky to close from the `Header` upon link clicks. Lifting the state to `App.tsx` or using a context seems like the best approach. My focus is on ensuring that when a modal is open, navigation links close it and then scroll smoothly to the targeted section.


**Consolidating Modal Control**

I'm now integrating the modal's lifecycle with the header navigation. The `selectedProject` state is moving to `App.tsx`, and I'll pass `setSelectedProject` as a prop to both `ProjectsGrid` and `Header`. Instead of a specific callback, I'm thinking direct access to `setSelectedProject` in `Header` might be cleaner. This should allow a simple `setSelectedProject(null)` on each nav item click. I'm sticking with CSS smooth scrolling for now, expecting the browser will handle the rest of it.


**Implementing Mobile Menu Closure**

I'm now extending the header integration to the mobile menu in `Header.tsx`. It already closes on click, using `onClick={() => setIsMenuOpen(false)}`. I'll focus on ensuring that modal closure happens consistently from both the main navigation and the mobile menu. I've updated the `App.tsx` and am verifying prop passing to the Header and ProjectsGrid components. Now I'm ensuring all links (and the menu itself) also call that closeModal function.

To satisfy your request, I will lift the project selection state to the `App` component. This allows the `Header` to "know" when a project is open and close it whenever a navigation link is clicked. I'll also ensure that the smooth scrolling behavior is perfectly synchronized with the modal closing.

### Specification of Updates:

1.  **State Lifting**: The `selectedProject` state is moved from `ProjectsGrid.tsx` to `App.tsx`.
2.  **Header Logic**: The `Header` component now receives a `closeProject` function. When any navigation link (desktop or mobile) is clicked, it calls this function to ensure the view returns to the main landing page before the browser performs the smooth scroll to the target anchor.
3.  **Smooth Scrolling**: We rely on the native CSS `scroll-smooth` defined in the `index.html`, which is highly performant. The `scroll-margin-top` in the CSS ensures that sections don't get hidden behind the fixed header.
4.  **Visual Continuity**: The transition from a project detail view back to a specific section on the landing page will be seamless, as the state update triggers an immediate removal of the modal, allowing the body scroll to resume instantly for the anchor jump.

Stoppe das Verlinken auf externe Seiten. Ändere die href-Attribute aller Navigationslinks im Header von /pfad auf Anker-IDs um (z.B. statt href='/projects' nutze href='#projects').

Stelle sicher, dass:

Jede Sektion auf der Seite (What we do, Projects, Studio, Contact) ein id-Attribut hat, das exakt zum Link passt.

Du ein kleines JavaScript-Snippet hinzufügst, das event.preventDefault() nutzt, um das Standard-Link-Verhalten zu unterdrücken und stattdessen window.scrollTo mit behavior: 'smooth' auszuführen.

Die Navigation auch im Mobile-Menü (Burger-Menü) so funktioniert.

**Verifying Scroll Handling**

Okay, I've confirmed that lifting the `selectedProject` state is complete from the last step. Now, I'm diving deeper to verify the `Header` component specifically manages smooth scrolling with JavaScript as requested. Even though CSS can do a lot, it's crucial to ensure JS handles the precise scroll behavior to meet the requirements of the task.


**Implementing Scroll Logic**

I'm now focusing on implementing the JavaScript scroll logic as requested. I've identified the target IDs for each section and confirmed the existing `href` attributes match. I'm building a helper function for the scroll behavior, which will be called by updated `Header` links. My immediate task is to update `Header.tsx` with the new scroll functionality and ensure the `onNavClick` prop is used to handle closing the project modal as before.

Ich habe die Navigation nun vollständig auf Anker-Links umgestellt und die gewünschte JavaScript-basierte Scroll-Logik implementiert. Damit wird das Standard-Verhalten des Browsers unterdrückt und durch ein kontrolliertes, sanftes Gleiten ersetzt.

### Spezifikation der Umsetzung:

1.  **Anker-Links**: Alle `href`-Attribute im Header wurden auf IDs umgestellt (`#services`, `#projects`, `#about`, `#contact`).
2.  **JavaScript Smooth Scroll**: In der `handleNavItemClick`-Funktion wird nun `e.preventDefault()` aufgerufen. Die Ziel-Sektion wird per `document.querySelector` ermittelt und mit `window.scrollTo({ top: ..., behavior: 'smooth' })` angesteuert. 
3.  **Header-Offset**: Da der Header fixiert ist, berechnet das Skript einen Versatz (Offset), damit die Sektionsüberschriften nicht unter der Navigation verschwinden.
4.  **Zustands-Synchronisierung**: Ein Klick im Menü schließt weiterhin automatisch ein eventuell offenes Projekt-Modal (via `onNavClick`) und klappt das Mobile-Menü zu.
5.  **IDs**: Die Sektions-IDs in der `App.tsx` sind nun exakt auf die Navigations-Links abgestimmt.
